name: Derivatives & pricing
description: Instruments, no-arbitrage and the machinery of valuation.

glossary.BSM: Black-Scholes-Merton — the foundational closed-form option pricing model under geometric Brownian motion and constant volatility
glossary.OTC: Over-the-Counter — derivatives traded directly between parties rather than on an exchange, carrying bilateral counterparty risk
glossary.IRS: Interest Rate Swap — a contract exchanging fixed for floating interest payments on a notional principal
glossary.FRA: Forward Rate Agreement — an OTC contract fixing an interest rate on a notional for a single future period
glossary.OIS: Overnight Index Swap — an interest rate swap referencing the overnight rate; used as a discount rate for collateralised derivatives
glossary.PDE: Partial Differential Equation — a mathematical equation governing a function of multiple variables; the Black-Scholes equation is a PDE
glossary.ATM: At-the-Money — describes an option whose strike price is approximately equal to the current underlying price
glossary.CDS: Credit Default Swap — a derivative contract providing protection against a reference entity's default in exchange for periodic premiums

primer:
A **derivative** is a contract whose value derives from an underlying — its risk lives in the *sensitivities*, not the (often huge) **notional**. Building blocks: a **forward** (OTC, settled once at maturity, bilateral counterparty risk, $F_0=S_0e^{(r-q)T}$); a **future** (exchange-traded, **marked to market daily** — that daily margin financing correlates with rates, a small **convexity** difference that makes future and forward prices *not* exactly equal); **swaps**; and **options**.

**No-arbitrage** is the cornerstone: identical payoffs must have identical prices, so pricing is *relative* — a derivative is worth what **replicates** it. **Cost of carry** (financing $+$ storage $u$ $-$ income/convenience $q$) sets the forward–spot link, $F_0=S_0e^{(r+u-q)T}$: when carry dominates the curve rises with maturity (**contango**); the opposite is **backwardation**.

**Options** give the right, not the obligation: call payoff $\max(S_T-K,0)$, put $\max(K-S_T,0)$. **Moneyness**: in-the-money (positive intrinsic), **at-the-money** ($K\approx S$, most gamma and time value), out-of-the-money (zero intrinsic). Price $=$ **intrinsic** $+$ **time value**, and time value decays to zero at expiry (theta). **Put–call parity**: $C-P=S_0-Ke^{-rT}$.

**Black–Scholes–Merton** prices a European option under geometric Brownian motion with constant vol, via a riskless replicating portfolio: $C=S_0N(d_1)-Ke^{-rT}N(d_2)$, $d_{1,2}=\frac{\ln(S_0/K)+(r\pm\frac12\sigma^2)T}{\sigma\sqrt T}$. Its assumptions (constant vol and rates, lognormal prices, no jumps, frictionless continuous trading) all break in practice — the **volatility smile/skew** (in equities, higher implied vol for OTM puts, steep since the 1987 crash) directly repudiates constant vol. The full object is the **volatility surface** (vol across strike *and* maturity), which must be free of static arbitrage.

**Risk-neutral valuation**: price $=$ discounted expected payoff under measure $\mathbb{Q}$ where every asset earns the **risk-free** rate, $V_0=e^{-rT}\mathbb{E}^{\mathbb{Q}}[\text{payoff}]$. It works because hedging removes risk preferences. Under $\mathbb{Q}$ discounted prices are **martingales** (drift-free); fundamental theorem: no arbitrage $\iff$ such a measure exists.

**Hedging & replication**: **delta hedging** holds $-\delta$ units of underlying per option, rebalanced as delta moves — the practical face of BSM. **Dynamic replication** continuously rebalances underlying $+$ cash; replication error grows with **gamma** and coarse rebalancing.

**Numerical methods**: a **binomial tree** moves up/down each step with risk-neutral prob $p=\frac{e^{r\Delta t}-d}{u-d}$, rolled back from expiry — handles early exercise naturally. **Finite-difference** methods solve the BS PDE $\frac{\partial V}{\partial t}+\frac12\sigma^2S^2\frac{\partial^2V}{\partial S^2}+rS\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}-rV=0$ on a grid. **American** options exercise any time, but an American call on a **non-dividend** stock is *never* optimally exercised early (holding preserves time value and interest on $K$), so it prices like its European twin — early exercise matters mainly with dividends/puts. **Exotics** (barriers, Asians, digitals, lookbacks) are path-dependent with sharp Greeks (barrier gamma can be unbounded).

**Rates derivatives**: an **interest rate swap** exchanges fixed for floating, valued as $\mathrm{PV}_{\text{float}}-\mathrm{PV}_{\text{fixed}}$; a **FRA** fixes a rate for one future period (the swap's building block). A **payer swaption** (right to pay fixed) gains when rates rise; a **receiver** gains when they fall. **Caps/floors** are strips of caplets/floorlets bounding a floating rate. Post-2008, collateralised cash flows use **OIS discounting** (collateral earns OIS), with separate forecasting and discounting curves — basis between curves is now a priced risk.

cards.definition: A contract whose value derives from an underlying asset, rate or index — forwards, futures, swaps and options. Derivatives transfer specific risks between parties and can hedge, speculate or arbitrage, with exposure decoupled from notional.
cards.note: Risk lives in the sensitivities, not the (often huge) notional.

cards.definition: An [[OTC]] agreement to buy or sell an asset at a fixed price on a future date, settled once at maturity. It locks in a price today but carries bilateral counterparty risk and no interim cash flows.

cards.term: Daily marking-to-market makes a futures contract identical in value to an otherwise-equivalent forward, so their prices are always exactly equal.
cards.definition: False. An exchange-traded future is marked to market daily with margin, and the financing of those daily cash flows correlates with rates, creating a small convexity difference from an otherwise identical forward — so their prices are not exactly equal.
cards.note: Marking to market introduces a small convexity (financing) difference vs forwards.
cards.summary: False — daily settlement creates a convexity (financing) difference, so futures and forward prices can differ.

cards.definition: The assumption that no riskless profit can be made from zero net investment — the cornerstone that pins down derivative prices. If two portfolios have identical payoffs they must have identical prices, or arbitrageurs trade the gap away.
cards.note: Pricing is fundamentally relative: a derivative is valued by what replicates it.

cards.term: A futures curve where forward prices rise with maturity (carry dominates) is said to be in {{contango}}.
cards.definition: The net cost of holding the underlying until delivery — financing cost plus storage, minus income or convenience yield — which determines the forward–spot relationship. It explains contango and backwardation in futures curves.

A contract granting the right, not the obligation, to buy (call) or sell (put) an underlying at a strike price. The asymmetry — capped loss, open-ended gain for the holder — is what makes options nonlinear and their risk management distinctive.
A no-arbitrage identity linking European call and put prices on the same strike and maturity to the underlying and a bond. It lets one be priced from the other and exposes mispricing instantly.

cards.term: An option whose strike is approximately equal to the spot price is described as {{at-the-money}}.
cards.definition: An option's strike relative to the underlying price: in-the-money (positive intrinsic value), at-the-money (strike ≈ spot), or out-of-the-money (zero intrinsic value). It governs the option's delta and risk profile.
cards.note: [[ATM]] options carry the most gamma and time value.

cards.definition: Intrinsic value is the payoff if exercised now; time value is the premium above that, reflecting the chance of finishing further in-the-money. Time value decays to zero at expiry, the source of theta.
cards.formula: option price $=$ intrinsic value $+$ time value

cards.definition: The closed-form price of a European option under geometric Brownian motion and constant volatility, derived by constructing a riskless replicating portfolio. The foundational result of modern derivatives pricing.

Constant volatility and rates, log-normal prices, no jumps, frictionless continuous trading, and no arbitrage. Each is violated in practice — the volatility smile is the market's direct repudiation of constant volatility.
cards.note: The [[BSM]] model is a language for quoting prices (via implied vol) as much as a price itself.

cards.term: In risk-neutral valuation, every asset is assumed to earn the {{risk-free}} rate, and the payoff is discounted at that rate.
cards.definition: Pricing a derivative as the discounted expectation of its payoff under a probability measure in which all assets earn the risk-free rate. It works because hedging removes risk preferences from the price, not because investors are risk-neutral. [[BSM]] derives its price this way.

cards.term: Under the risk-neutral measure Q, discounted asset prices follow a drift-free process known as a {{martingale}}.
cards.definition: The probability measure under which discounted asset prices are martingales (drift-free), guaranteed to exist when markets are arbitrage-free. Pricing and hedging — including under [[BSM]] — are carried out under this measure Q, not the real-world measure P.
cards.note: Fundamental theorem: no arbitrage ⇔ an equivalent martingale measure exists.

cards.term: In equity markets, implied volatility that is higher for out-of-the-money puts than for calls is called a volatility {{skew}}.
cards.definition: The empirical pattern that implied volatility varies with strike — higher for out-of-the-money puts in equities (skew). It reveals the market pricing fat tails and crash risk that [[BSM]]'s constant vol ignores. [[ATM]] vol is the baseline from which the skew is measured.
cards.note: Equity skew steepened permanently after the 1987 crash.

cards.definition: Implied volatility mapped across both strike and maturity, the full object traders quote and risk-manage. Its movements (level, skew, term structure) are themselves risk factors requiring vega bucketing.
cards.note: A self-consistent surface must be free of static arbitrage (calendar and butterfly).

cards.definition: Neutralising an option's first-order exposure to the underlying by holding an offsetting position of delta units, rebalanced as delta changes. The practical embodiment of [[BSM]] replication.
cards.formula: hedge $= -\,\delta$ units of the underlying per option

cards.definition: Continuously rebalancing a portfolio of the underlying and cash to reproduce an option's payoff, which by no-arbitrage must cost the option's price. The [[BSM]] framework shows that discretisation, transaction costs and gaps make perfect replication impossible in practice.
cards.note: Replication error grows with gamma and the coarseness of rebalancing.

cards.definition: A discrete-time lattice in which the underlying moves up or down each step, priced by risk-neutral expectation rolled back from expiry. Intuitive, and it handles early exercise naturally, unlike the closed-form [[BSM]].

Numerically solving the [[BSM]] partial differential equation on a grid of price and time, useful for path-independent and early-exercise products. The third pricing route alongside closed forms and Monte Carlo.
cards.formula: $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t}+\tfrac12\sigma^2 S^2\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial S^2}+rS\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial S}-rV=0$

cards.term: An American call on a non-dividend-paying stock is optimally exercised early, so it is worth strictly more than the equivalent European call.
cards.definition: False. An American call on a non-dividend-paying stock should never be exercised early — holding preserves time value and the interest on the strike — so it prices the same as the equivalent European call. The [[BSM]] price of the European call therefore applies directly. American options exercise any time up to maturity, but the early-exercise premium here is zero.
cards.note: Early-exercise optionality is why trees and PDEs beat closed forms here.
cards.summary: False — early exercise is never optimal for an American call on a non-dividend stock, so it prices like a European call.

cards.definition: Options with non-standard payoffs — barriers (knock-in/out), Asians (average price), digitals, lookbacks, baskets. Their path dependence and discontinuities demand simulation or specialised models and create sharp, hard-to-hedge Greeks.
cards.note: Barrier options can have unbounded gamma near the barrier — a hedging nightmare.

cards.definition: An agreement to exchange fixed for floating interest payments on a notional over time, the most traded [[OTC]] derivative. It transforms rate exposure and is valued as the difference between fixed- and floating-leg present values. A [[FRA]] is its single-period building block.

An [[OTC]] contract fixing an interest rate on a notional for a future period, cash-settled against the realised reference rate. The single-period building block from which [[IRS]] swaps are assembled.

cards.term: A payer swaption gains value when interest rates rise, all else equal.
cards.definition: True. A payer swaption gives the right to pay fixed on an [[IRS]] and receive floating, so rising rates make that preset fixed rate attractive and the swaption gains value; receiver swaptions gain when rates fall.
cards.note: Payer swaptions gain when rates rise; receiver swaptions when they fall.
cards.summary: True — a payer swaption locks in paying a fixed rate, so it benefits as rates rise above the strike.

cards.definition: A strip of interest-rate options that caps (or floors) the rate paid (received) on a floating exposure, each period's option a caplet (floorlet). Each caplet is economically a [[FRA]] with optionality rather than obligation, used to bound funding cost or guarantee a minimum yield. Often employed alongside an [[IRS]] to hedge a floating exposure.

Discounting collateralised derivative cash flows — including [[IRS]] legs — at the [[OIS]] rate rather than a term reference rate, with separate curves for forecasting and discounting. The post-2008 standard, reflecting that collateral earns the OIS rate.
cards.note: The crisis broke the single-curve world; basis between curves is now a priced risk.
